note: Curated AI/GPU/ML/Agents/DataScience facts. Used as the offline fallback for the Glossary flipbook page. Fetched client-side; a remote API can be substituted by editing kb/glossary.html (the FACTS_URL constant).

facts.text: GPT-3 has 175 billion parameters; training it once cost an estimated $4.6M in compute.
An H100 GPU has 80GB of HBM3 memory with 3.35 TB/s of bandwidth — about 30x faster than typical DDR5 system RAM.
The Transformer paper 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017) replaced recurrence with self-attention and is now cited over 100,000 times.
KV cache memory for a single Llama-70B request can exceed 40GB at long context lengths — often the actual bottleneck, not the model weights.
An NVIDIA Blackwell B200 packs 208 billion transistors across two reticle-limit dies fused with a 10 TB/s NV-HBI interconnect.
FlashAttention reduces attention memory from O(N²) to O(N) by tiling and never materialising the full attention matrix in HBM.
The MCP (Model Context Protocol) specification, released by Anthropic in late 2024, lets any LLM client talk to any tool server using JSON-RPC over stdio or HTTP.
BF16 (bfloat16) keeps the same 8-bit exponent as FP32 — preserving dynamic range — while truncating the mantissa to 7 bits. This is why it 'just works' for training.
AlphaGo's 2016 win over Lee Sedol used 1,920 CPUs and 280 GPUs for distributed Monte Carlo Tree Search.
PagedAttention (vLLM) treats KV cache like virtual memory pages, eliminating fragmentation and enabling 2-4x higher serving throughput.
A single training step of GPT-4 reportedly used around 25,000 A100 GPUs running in parallel for roughly 90 days.
ReAct prompting (Reason + Act, 2022) lets an LLM interleave thoughts and tool calls — the architectural basis of most modern agents.
Tenstorrent's Wormhole architecture uses RISC-V baby cores and a 2D mesh of Tensix cores — no monolithic SIMT scheduler like CUDA.
ImageNet, the dataset that arguably launched the deep learning revolution, contains 14 million hand-labeled images across 22,000 categories.
The original Transformer used 8 attention heads. Llama-3-70B uses 64 query heads and 8 KV heads (grouped-query attention).
FP8 inference (E4M3 / E5M2) cuts memory bandwidth pressure roughly 2x vs FP16 — the main reason it's the default on Hopper and Blackwell.
Mixture-of-Experts models like Mixtral 8x7B have 47B total parameters but activate only ~13B per token — the cost of a much smaller dense model.
Tool use in agents is dominated by a single failure mode: the model hallucinates an argument the schema didn't declare. Strict JSON schemas reduce this by an order of magnitude.
An H100 SXM5 draws 700W under load — more than a midrange gaming PC's entire power supply.
Speculative decoding can speed up autoregressive generation by 2-3x by having a small 'draft' model propose tokens that the large model verifies in parallel.
The 'attention is all you need' paper was rejected from NeurIPS 2017 spotlight track. It now defines the architecture behind essentially every modern LLM.
Random forests were proposed by Leo Breiman in 2001 and remain the strongest baseline for tabular data — even today, against deep learning.
Training Llama-3-405B used 30.84 million GPU-hours on H100s — equivalent to ~3,500 GPU-years.
The GPU was invented by Sony for the PlayStation in 1994. NVIDIA didn't ship the first 'GPU' branded chip (the GeForce 256) until 1999.
Function calling, structured output, and tool use are three names for essentially the same capability: constrained decoding against a JSON schema.
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) was named in a 2020 Meta paper — but the idea of conditioning generation on retrieved documents is much older.
An NVIDIA NVLink connection between two H100s runs at 900 GB/s — roughly the same bandwidth as a 2015-era HBM2 stack.
Modern LLMs are typically trained on between 1 and 15 trillion tokens. The entire English Wikipedia is about 4 billion tokens — a rounding error.
DeepSeek-V3 was trained for a reported $5.5M in compute — using 2048 H800 GPUs for ~2 months.
k-nearest-neighbours is still the most-cited algorithm in machine learning education, despite being published in 1951 by Fix and Hodges.
The 'temperature' parameter in LLM sampling is literally borrowed from Boltzmann statistics: lower temperature → sharper distribution → more deterministic output.
An agent loop with no exit condition will happily burn $100 of API credit in an hour. This is the #1 cause of LLM cost overruns in production.
The first published convolutional neural network (LeNet-5, 1998) had 60,000 parameters. GPT-4 has roughly 30 million times more.
Tensor parallelism splits matrix multiplications across GPUs along the inner dimension; pipeline parallelism splits along the depth of layers. Most large models combine both.
The Adam optimizer was published in 2014 and has been the default for training neural networks ever since — despite numerous claims of replacement.
Apple Silicon's unified memory architecture means a single 192GB tensor can fit on an M2 Ultra. No HBM/PCIe transfer ever happens during inference.
Constitutional AI (Anthropic, 2022) trains models to critique and revise their own outputs against a written set of principles — no human preference labels required.
A single inference request through GPT-4 uses an estimated 0.001 - 0.01 kWh of energy. Multiply by 100M users/day and you get the energy footprint of a small country.
The phrase 'prompt engineering' first appeared in published research around 2021. By 2024 it had its own job title with $200K+ salaries.
Hugging Face hosts over 1 million models. The vast majority have fewer than 10 downloads — a long tail dominated by fine-tunes and experiments.
